title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: easyGSEA supports custom pathway annotations non-model organisms don’t pre-built annotation databases (org.*.eg.db packages). vignette shows create use custom pathway files.

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: Use custom pathways : - Working non-model organisms (e.g., Bradysia, emerging model systems) - Standard organism databases don’t exist - species-specific pathway annotations - want use custom gene sets (e.g., literature) Don’t need custom pathways : - organism : human, mouse, rat, fly, zebrafish, worm, yeast - Use organism = "auto" instead (easier!)

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: easyGSEA accepts two formats:

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: Simple two-column format: Requirements: - Header row columns: pathway gene_id - One gene per row - Genes can appear multiple pathways - Pathway names: use underscores (spaces) - Gene IDs: match DESeq2 rownames EXACTLY
code: pathway,gene_id DNA_repair,gene_001 DNA_repair,gene_002 DNA_repair,gene_005 Cell_cycle,gene_003 Cell_cycle,gene_004 Cell_cycle,gene_007 Translation,gene_006 Translation,gene_008

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: Standard gene set format: Format: - Tab-separated - Column 1: Pathway name - Column 2: Description (can “NA”) - Column 3+: Gene IDs
code: DNA_repair  description gene_001    gene_002    gene_005 Cell_cycle  description gene_003    gene_004    gene_007 Translation description gene_006    gene_008

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: gene IDs must match DESeq2 rownames:
code: library(DESeq2)  # Check your gene IDs head(rownames(dds)) # Example output: "Brad_gene_001" "Brad_gene_002" ...

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: Option : Use Orthology Map genes model organism, inherit pathways: Option B: Literature-Based Manually curate publications: Option C: Functional Annotation Use annotation tools:
code: # 1. Find orthologs (e.g., using BLAST or OrthoFinder) # 2. Get pathways for model organism orthologs # 3. Transfer pathways to your genes  # Example result: # Your_gene_001 → Drosophila FBgn123 → GO:0006281 (DNA repair) # Create data frame pathways <- data.frame(   pathway = c("DNA_repair", "DNA_repair", "Cell_cycle"),   gene_id = c("Brad_gene_001", "Brad_gene_002", "Brad_gene_003") )  # Save as CSV write.csv(pathways, "bradysia_pathways.csv", row.names = FALSE) # Tools to consider: # - InterProScan (protein domains) # - eggNOG-mapper (orthology + GO terms) # - BLAST2GO (BLAST + GO mapping) # - Trinotate (for transcriptomes)  # These generate GO terms you can convert to pathway file

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: Real example non-model organism research:

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
code: library(easyGSEA) library(DESeq2)  # Load your DESeq2 object # Genes are named: Brad_01, Brad_02, etc. head(rownames(dds))

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: Using orthology Drosophila:
code: # Find Drosophila orthologs (external tool: OrthoFinder, BLAST) # Map Drosophila genes to GO terms # Transfer GO terms to Bradysia genes  # Example pathway file: bradysia_go <- data.frame(   pathway = c(     "GO:0007049", "GO:0007049", "GO:0007049",  # Cell cycle     "GO:0006281", "GO:0006281",                 # DNA repair     "GO:0006412", "GO:0006412", "GO:0006412"   # Translation   ),   gene_id = c(     "Brad_001", "Brad_045", "Brad_089",     "Brad_012", "Brad_067",     "Brad_023", "Brad_098", "Brad_134"   ) )  # Save write.csv(bradysia_go, "bradysia_pathways.csv", row.names = FALSE)

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
code: # Run with custom pathways results <- gsea_from_deseq(   dds = dds,   contrast = c("stage", "germline", "soma"),   custom_pathways = "bradysia_pathways.csv" )  # Results are same format as standard GSEA nrow(results) head(results@result[, c("Description", "NES", "p.adjust")])

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
code: # Same plotting functions work plot_gsea(results)  # Compare developmental stages stage1 <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                           contrast = c("stage", "stage1", "control"),                          custom_pathways = "bradysia_pathways.csv")                           stage2 <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                          contrast = c("stage", "stage2", "control"),                          custom_pathways = "bradysia_pathways.csv")  comparison <- compare_gsea(stage1, stage2,                            label1 = "Stage 1", label2 = "Stage 2") plot_comparison(comparison)

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
code: # Read your file pathways <- read.csv("my_pathways.csv")  # Check format colnames(pathways)  # Should be: "pathway" "gene_id"  # Check gene IDs match DESeq2 gene_overlap <- intersect(pathways$gene_id, rownames(dds)) coverage <- length(gene_overlap) / nrow(dds) * 100  cat("Gene coverage:", round(coverage, 1), "%\n") # Aim for >70% coverage

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
code: # Count genes per pathway pathway_sizes <- table(pathways$pathway)  # Check distribution summary(as.numeric(pathway_sizes))  # Recommendations: # - Minimum: 10 genes per pathway (smaller = unreliable) # - Maximum: 500 genes per pathway (larger = too broad) # - Ideal: 20-200 genes per pathway

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: Issue 1: pathways found Issue 2: pathways Need pathway annotations Try relaxing padj_cutoff: padj_cutoff = 0.1 Check knockdown strong enough Issue 3: pathways enriched Pathway file small/broad Integration artifacts dominating Check replicate comparisons
code: # Check gene ID matching setdiff(pathways$gene_id[1:10], rownames(dds)) # Should be empty. If not, IDs don't match!

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
code: # Use Trinity gene IDs directly # GENE_001, GENE_002, etc.  # Annotate with Trinotate # Extract GO terms # Convert to pathway file

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
code: # Use official gene IDs # e.g., LOC genes, maker annotations  # Use eggNOG-mapper for GO terms # Download results # Parse into pathway file

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
code: # Use human/mouse gene IDs # But create custom pathways for: # - Cell-type specific processes # - Disease-relevant pathways # - Literature-curated gene sets

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: Orthology Tools: - OrthoFinder: https://github.com/davidemms/OrthoFinder - eggNOG-mapper: http://eggnog-mapper.embl.de/ - OrthoDB: https://www.orthodb.org/ Functional Annotation: - InterProScan: https://www.ebi.ac.uk/interpro/ - Trinotate: https://github.com/Trinotate/Trinotate - BLAST2GO: https://www.blast2go.com/ Gene Ontology: - GO database: http://geneontology.org/ - QuickGO: https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: See package GitHub example pathway files: - inst/extdata/bradysia_pathways.csv - inst/extdata/example_pathways.gmt

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: See vignette("quickstart") basic usage See vignette("comparison") comparing conditions Check ?gsea_from_deseq parameters

title: Custom Pathways for Non-Model Organisms
text: use easyGSEA custom pathways, please cite: easyGSEA: Samuel GH (2026). easyGSEA: Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis. R package version 0.1.0. clusterProfiler: Yu G, Wang LG, Han Y, QY (2012). “clusterProfiler: R package comparing biological themes among gene clusters.” OMICS, 16(5), 284-287.

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: easyGSEA simplifies Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) handling DESeq2 results directly. manual gene ID conversion, confusing p-value filtering, complex workflows.

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
code: # Install from GitHub devtools::install_github("ghsamuel/easyGSEA")  # Install organism databases (if needed) BiocManager::install("org.Hs.eg.db")  # Human BiocManager::install("org.Mm.eg.db")  # Mouse

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
code: library(DESeq2) library(easyGSEA)  # Assume you have a DESeqDataSet object dds <- DESeq(dds)

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
code: # Auto-detect organism and run GSEA results <- gsea_from_deseq(   dds = dds,   contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control") )  # See how many pathways found nrow(results)  # View top pathways head(results@result[, c("Description", "NES", "p.adjust")])

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
code: # Dotplot (default) plot_gsea(results)  # Barplot plot_gsea(results, type = "barplot")  # Save all plots plot_all(results, output_dir = "my_gsea_results")

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: common use case comparing two experimental conditions (e.g., two clones, two treatments, two time points).
code: # Run GSEA on both conditions condition1 <- gsea_from_deseq(dds, contrast = c("treatment", "A", "control")) condition2 <- gsea_from_deseq(dds, contrast = c("treatment", "B", "control"))  # Compare them comparison <- compare_gsea(condition1, condition2,                           label1 = "Treatment A",                           label2 = "Treatment B")  # View summary statistics comparison$summary  # View common pathways head(comparison$pathways[comparison$pathways$Status == "Common", ])  # Plot comparison plot_comparison(comparison)

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: comparison provides: Common pathways: Present conditions (highest confidence) Condition-specific pathways: May reflect real biological differences technical artifacts (-targets, integration sites, batch effects) Jaccard index: Similarity metric (0 = overlap, 1 = identical) Direction concordance: % common pathways going direction replicate/clone comparisons: High concordance (>80%) expected. Low overlap may indicate technical artifacts. different treatment comparisons: Low overlap expected - ’re comparing different biology!

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: default, easyGSEA auto-detects organism gene IDs: can also specify explicitly: Supported organisms: human, mouse, rat, fly, zebrafish, worm, yeast
code: # Auto-detect (recommended) results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds, contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control")) # Human results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                            contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control"),                           organism = "human")  # Mouse results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                           contrast = c("condition", "KO", "WT"),                           organism = "mouse")

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: Two ranking methods available:

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: Use : Standard RNA-seq analysis. Emphasizes biological effect size.
code: results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                           contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control"),                           ranking = "log2fc")

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: Use : Want emphasize statistical confidence. Finds pathways (including subtle consistent changes). Note: Wald ranking typically finds 2-3x pathways log2FC favors consistent changes even effect size moderate.
code: results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                           contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control"),                           ranking = "wald")

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: organisms without annotation databases, provide custom pathways: See vignette("custom-pathways") details.
code: # Create custom pathway file (CSV format) # Columns: pathway, gene_id # Example: "DNA_repair", "gene_001"  results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                           contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control"),                           custom_pathways = "my_pathways.csv")

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: easyGSEA always filters adjusted p-values (padj < 0.05 default), never nominal p-values. critical avoiding false positives testing thousands pathways.
code: # Change significance threshold if needed results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                           contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control"),                           padj_cutoff = 0.01)  # More stringent

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: Genes automatically converted ENSEMBL ENTREZ IDs. genes (~10-20%) may fail map - normal.

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: Typical ranges: - Weak perturbation: 10-50 pathways - Moderate perturbation: 100-400 pathways - Strong perturbation: 400-800 pathways - strong: 800+ pathways get >1000 pathways, consider using stringent threshold (padj_cutoff = 0.01).

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: See ?gsea_from_deseq full parameter details See vignette("model-organisms") organism-specific guidance See vignette("custom-pathways") non-model organisms See vignette("comparison") detailed comparison examples

title: Getting Started with easyGSEA
text: use easyGSEA, please cite package clusterProfiler: easyGSEA: Samuel GH (2026). easyGSEA: Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis. R package version 0.1.0. https://github.com/ghsamuel/easyGSEA clusterProfiler: Yu G, Wang LG, Han Y, QY (2012). “clusterProfiler: R package comparing biological themes among gene clusters.” OMICS: Journal Integrative Biology, 16(5), 284-287. doi:10.1089/omi.2011.0118

what: easyGSEA • Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
title: easyGSEA • Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis DESeq2 results.

title: easyGSEA • Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: Standard GSEA workflows confusing: - ❌ Filtering nominal p-values instead adjusted (common mistake!) - ❌ Complex gene ID conversion - ❌ easy way compare conditions - ❌ Unclear ranking method use easyGSEA solves simple interface right.

title: easyGSEA • Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
code: # Install from GitHub devtools::install_github("ghsamuel/easyGSEA")

title: easyGSEA • Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
code: library(easyGSEA)  # Run GSEA directly from DESeq2 results results <- gsea_from_deseq(   dds = dds,   contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control") )  # Plot results plot_all(results)  # Compare two conditions comparison <- compare_gsea(results_m1, results_m2) plot_comparison(comparison)

title: easyGSEA • Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: ✅ Works directly DESeq2 results (preprocessing needed) ✅ Auto-detects organism gene IDs ✅ Always uses adjusted p-values (never nominal) ✅ Supports custom pathways non-model organisms ✅ Easy condition comparison ✅ Publication-ready plots

title: easyGSEA • Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: 🚧 active development - check back soon!

what: Compare GSEA Results Between Two Conditions — compare_gsea
title: Compare GSEA Results Between Two Conditions — compare_gsea
text: Compares two GSEA results identify common condition-specific pathways. Calculates overlap statistics, direction concordance, identifies discordant pathways.

title: Compare GSEA Results Between Two Conditions — compare_gsea
code: compare_gsea(gsea1, gsea2, label1 = "Condition1", label2 = "Condition2")

title: Compare GSEA Results Between Two Conditions — compare_gsea
text: gsea1 gseaResult object first condition gsea2 gseaResult object second condition label1 Character string label first condition (default: "Condition1") label2 Character string label second condition (default: "Condition2")

title: Compare GSEA Results Between Two Conditions — compare_gsea
text: list containing: common_pathways Data frame pathways significant conditions specific1 Data frame pathways condition 1 specific2 Data frame pathways condition 2 stats List summary statistics (overlap, concordance, etc.) labels Named vector condition labels

title: Compare GSEA Results Between Two Conditions — compare_gsea
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Compare M1 vs M2 clones m1_gsea <- gsea_from_deseq(dds, contrast = c("clone", "M1", "S1")) m2_gsea <- gsea_from_deseq(dds, contrast = c("clone", "M2", "S1"))  comparison <- compare_gsea(m1_gsea, m2_gsea,                            label1 = "M1", label2 = "M2")  # View statistics comparison$stats  # View common pathways head(comparison$common_pathways) } # }

what: Run GSEA from DESeq2 Results — gsea_from_deseq
title: Run GSEA from DESeq2 Results — gsea_from_deseq
text: Simplified Gene Set Enrichment Analysis handles DESeq2 results directly. Automatically converts gene IDs, ranks genes, runs GSEA proper adjusted p-value filtering.

title: Run GSEA from DESeq2 Results — gsea_from_deseq
code: gsea_from_deseq(   dds,   contrast,   organism = "auto",   ranking = "log2fc",   custom_pathways = NULL,   padj_cutoff = 0.05,   ... )

title: Run GSEA from DESeq2 Results — gsea_from_deseq
text: dds DESeqDataSet object DESeq2 contrast Character vector specifying contrast, e.g. c("condition", "treated", "control") organism Character string specifying organism. Options: "auto" (default, auto-detect), "human", "mouse", "rat", "fly", "zebrafish", "worm", "yeast". Use "auto" detect gene IDs. ranking Character string specifying ranking method. Options: "log2fc" (default, rank log2 fold change) "wald" (rank Wald statistic). log2fc recommended standard analyses. custom_pathways Optional path custom pathway file (CSV GMT format) non-model organisms. See vignette format details. padj_cutoff Adjusted p-value cutoff pathway significance. Default: 0.05. Note: ALWAYS adjusted p-value, never nominal. ... Additional arguments passed clusterProfiler::gseGO clusterProfiler::GSEA

title: Run GSEA from DESeq2 Results — gsea_from_deseq
text: gseaResult object clusterProfiler containing significant pathways (padj < cutoff)

title: Run GSEA from DESeq2 Results — gsea_from_deseq
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Basic usage with auto-detection results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                            contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control"))  # Specify organism explicitly results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                            contrast = c("condition", "KO", "WT"),                            organism = "mouse")  # Use Wald statistic for ranking results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                            contrast = c("genotype", "mutant", "wildtype"),                            ranking = "wald")  # Non-model organism with custom pathways results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds,                            contrast = c("treatment", "treated", "control"),                            custom_pathways = "my_pathways.csv") } # }

what: Plot All Standard GSEA Visualizations — plot_all
title: Plot All Standard GSEA Visualizations — plot_all
text: Generate standard plots save directory.

title: Plot All Standard GSEA Visualizations — plot_all
code: plot_all(x, output_dir = "gsea_plots", format = "pdf", width = 12, height = 8)

title: Plot All Standard GSEA Visualizations — plot_all
text: x gseaResult object output_dir Directory save plots (default: "gsea_plots") format File format: "pdf" "png" (default: "pdf") width Plot width inches (default: 12) height Plot height inches (default: 8)

title: Plot All Standard GSEA Visualizations — plot_all
text: Invisible NULL (plots saved files)

title: Plot All Standard GSEA Visualizations — plot_all
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds, contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control")) plot_all(results, output_dir = "my_gsea_plots") } # }

what: Plot Comparison Between Two GSEA Results — plot_comparison
title: Plot Comparison Between Two GSEA Results — plot_comparison
text: Create side--side comparison plot showing common pathways two conditions.

title: Plot Comparison Between Two GSEA Results — plot_comparison
code: plot_comparison(comparison, top_n = 20, show_all = FALSE)

title: Plot Comparison Between Two GSEA Results — plot_comparison
text: comparison gsea_comparison object compare_gsea() top_n Number top common pathways show (default: 20) show_all Logical, TRUE shows pathways just common (default: FALSE)

title: Plot Comparison Between Two GSEA Results — plot_comparison
text: ggplot2 object

title: Plot Comparison Between Two GSEA Results — plot_comparison
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ comparison <- compare_gsea(gsea1, gsea2, label1 = "M1", label2 = "M2") plot_comparison(comparison) plot_comparison(comparison, top_n = 30) } # }

title: Plot GSEA Results — plot_gsea
text: Create publication-ready plots GSEA results.

title: Plot GSEA Results — plot_gsea
code: plot_gsea(x, type = "dotplot", top_n = 20, ...)

title: Plot GSEA Results — plot_gsea
text: x gseaResult object gsea_from_deseq() type Plot type: "dotplot" (default), "barplot", "enrichment" top_n Number top pathways show (default: 20) ... Additional arguments passed clusterProfiler plotting functions

title: Plot GSEA Results — plot_gsea
text: ggplot2 object

title: Plot GSEA Results — plot_gsea
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ results <- gsea_from_deseq(dds, contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control")) plot_gsea(results) plot_gsea(results, type = "barplot", top_n = 15) } # }
